meta.title: How UK charity status, tax, finance, control, public data and help routes fit together
coverage: The regulators and official register doors across all four UK nations, plus selected common legal forms with their jurisdiction shown.
Conditional tax treatments, reporting obligations, funding routes and organisation-level finance disclosures.
Stewardship and control models that avoid describing charities as equity-owned businesses.
A fail-closed collection and help-discovery pipeline for future organisation-level public data.
exclusions: No charity organisation records, curated examples or grant-recipient records in this version.
No people, trustee directory, biographies, work histories, named pay, personal contacts or home addresses.
No inferred beliefs, denominations, affiliations or cross-register person matching.
No claim that public availability alone makes republication lawful, fair or safe.
No legal, tax, accounting, safeguarding or funding advice for a particular matter.
editorialRules: Religion is a possible charitable purpose, not an automatic tax exemption; public benefit and the applicable legal tests still matter.
Charity-regulator registration and HMRC recognition for tax purposes are related but not interchangeable.
Every tax relief is described with its conditions and failure path; VAT is never described as a blanket charity exemption.
Trustees steward charitable assets under duties; they are not described as beneficial equity owners.
Pay is represented only through organisation-level filed totals or bands, never through a named-person salary profile.
Official identifiers may support exact organisation joins in a future reviewed dataset; names never establish identity.
Source rights, privacy, special-category risk and field admission are checked before collection, and uncertainty stops publication.
TaxSorted analysis is labelled as analysis rather than presented as legislation or regulator policy.
meta.warning: A source-linked map of the system, not a shadow charity register. Follow the official source and current rules before acting.

sources.title: Charities Act 2011
supports: The statutory meanings of charity, charitable purpose and public benefit in England and Wales.
The legal framework for registration, reporting and the Charity Commission.
doesNotProve: That a purpose described in section 3 is automatically charitable without public benefit.
The tax treatment or facts of any particular organisation.

sources.title: Charitable purposes
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: Each charitable purpose must be for the public benefit; there is no automatic presumption.
Advancement of religion is one description of purpose and is assessed under charity law.
doesNotProve: That any belief system, religious activity or organisation passes the legal tests.
That charity law endorses a belief.

sources.title: Public benefit: rules for charities
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: Charities must have only charitable purposes for public benefit and carry them out for public benefit.
Trustees report how the charity carried out its purposes for public benefit in the trustees' annual report.
doesNotProve: That the regulator has verified every reported benefit.
That a purpose description creates an automatic presumption.

sources.title: Set up a charity: structures
sources.publisher: GOV.UK and Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: The common charitable company, CIO, trust and unincorporated association structures.
How structure affects legal personality, contracts, property and liability.
doesNotProve: Which structure is suitable for a particular organisation.
That trustees beneficially own charity assets.

sources.title: Charity types: how to choose a structure (CC22a)
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: Detailed differences between CIOs, charitable companies, trusts and unincorporated associations.
How incorporation, membership, contracts, liability and property holding differ by structure.
doesNotProve: Which form a particular group should choose.
That a governance member or legal title holder beneficially owns charitable assets.

sources.title: Excepted charities
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: Excepted charities remain subject to charity law while being excepted from registration and annual returns.
The time-limited registration exception for qualifying linked church charities with annual income of £100,000 or less ends on 31 March 2031.
doesNotProve: That every church or place of worship is a charity or is excepted.
That excepted and exempt charities mean the same thing.

sources.title: Charities and tax: overview and tax reliefs
supports: HMRC recognition is required for charity tax reliefs.
Most income and gains can be relieved when used for charitable purposes, while non-charitable expenditure can create tax.
doesNotProve: A blanket exemption from every tax.
That regulator registration alone establishes HMRC recognition.

sources.title: Charities and tax: get recognition for tax relief
supports: The conditions and application route for HMRC recognition as a charity for tax purposes.
doesNotProve: That HMRC recognition replaces registration with an applicable charity regulator.
That a pending applicant may claim every relief.

sources.title: Charities and tax: pay tax
supports: A charity files when it has non-qualifying income or HMRC asks, including when no tax is expected after a notice.
The return type differs for charitable trusts and other charity bodies, with applicable deadlines and late-filing consequences.
doesNotProve: The confidential filing status, liability or deadline of a particular charity.
That a charity-regulator return replaces an HMRC return.

sources.title: Annex i: tax exemptions for charities
supports: The main Income Tax and Corporation Tax exemptions for charitable trusts and companies.
Conditional treatment of income, investments, property, trading and capital gains.
doesNotProve: That every receipt is exempt or that application to charitable purposes can be assumed.
The amount of tax due in a particular case.

sources.title: Annex ii: non-charitable expenditure
supports: How non-charitable expenditure restricts exemptions and can require a return and tax payment.
doesNotProve: That a particular payment is non-charitable without applying the rules and facts.

sources.title: Charities and trading
sources.publisher: HM Revenue & Customs and Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: Primary-purpose, ancillary and small non-primary-purpose trading rules.
The role and limits of a separate trading subsidiary.
doesNotProve: That calling an activity fundraising or putting it in a subsidiary removes all tax and charity-law duties.

sources.title: VAT for charities: registration
supports: Charities are not generally exempt from VAT and must register when taxable turnover exceeds the current threshold.
Specific supplies and purchases can receive specific VAT treatment.
doesNotProve: That a charity can recover all input VAT or that all its supplies are exempt.

sources.title: Claiming Gift Aid as a charity or CASC: what you can claim it on
supports: HMRC recognition, an eligible individual donation and a donor declaration are conditions for a Gift Aid claim.
Some payments and donations do not qualify.
doesNotProve: That every donation or donor qualifies.
Any individual donor identity or donation.

sources.title: Business rates relief: charitable rate relief
supports: Eligible charitable use can receive up to 80% mandatory relief in England and a council may grant discretionary relief.
doesNotProve: A zero bill, entitlement for an unused property or the rules in Scotland, Wales or Northern Ireland.

sources.title: SDLTM26005: charities relief
supports: Conditional SDLT relief for a charity or charitable trust purchasing land.
Circumstances in which relief can be withdrawn after a disqualifying event.
doesNotProve: That every charity property purchase qualifies or that SDLT applies in Scotland or Wales.

sources.title: Prepare a charity annual return
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: The current annual-return deadline and reporting bands in England and Wales.
The accounts, independent-examination and audit requirements shown by the regulator on the review date.
doesNotProve: Which thresholds apply after a future effective date without rechecking current guidance.
That filed accounts contain every asset, outcome or individual payment a reader may want.

sources.title: Changes to charity accounting and reporting
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: The regulator says announced financial-reporting threshold changes are expected to take effect on 30 September 2026 for accounting years ending on or after that date.
SORP 2026 applies to reporting periods starting on or after 1 January 2026.
doesNotProve: That expected threshold changes are already legally in force before their effective date.
Which rule applies after 30 September 2026 without checking the enacted law and refreshed guidance.

sources.title: Full register download
sources.publisher: Charity Commission for England and Wales
supports: A daily JSON and tab-delimited extract of public register tables and their data definition.
Organisation, classification, return, finance, policy, report, governing-document and trustee tables exist.
doesNotProve: That every charity is registered or that every published field is safe and lawful to republish in a new service.
A complete view of work, impact, assets, pay or control.

sources.title: Find charity information
supports: The territorial route to Charity Commission, OSCR and CCNI register information.
doesNotProve: That one register covers the whole UK or that every charity is registered.

sources.title: Download the Scottish Charity Register
sources.publisher: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator

licence.name: Open Government Licence v3.0
licence.attribution: © Crown Copyright and database right [year]. Contains information from the Scottish Charity Register supplied by OSCR and licensed under the Open Government Licence v3.0.

supports: OSCR offers a daily full-register download, five years of annual-return data and a beta API application route.
Reuse conditions include attribution, no direct marketing and no claim that a derived list is the Scottish Charity Register.
doesNotProve: Continuous technical availability of a download endpoint.
Permission to create a marketing list or copy sensitive material from filed documents.

sources.title: Charity accounting and annual reporting
sources.publisher: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator
supports: Every registered Scottish charity submits an annual return, accounts, trustees' annual report and external scrutiny report within nine months.
Accounting form varies with income, legal form and other requirements.
doesNotProve: That all accounting figures are comparable across charities or years.
That a late or missing return proves misconduct beyond non-compliance with reporting.

sources.title: What is a Scottish Charitable Incorporated Organisation?
sources.publisher: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator
supports: A SCIO is a Scottish incorporated legal entity regulated by OSCR and can transact in its own name.
Its trustees are generally protected from personal liability for its transactions.
doesNotProve: That trustees or members hold equity in SCIO assets.
That a SCIO is the correct form for a particular plan.

sources.title: Charities (Regulation and Administration) (Scotland) Act 2023 changes
sources.publisher: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator
supports: From 9 March 2026 OSCR publishes submitted annual reports and accounts for five years in the format received.
OSCR tells charities to minimise unnecessary personal material and use typed or digital rather than handwritten signatures.
doesNotProve: That every item inside a filed PDF is suitable for extraction and republication.
That public filing removes data-protection duties.

sources.title: What happens when a charity does not submit on time?
sources.publisher: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator
supports: Late annual information is treated as defaulting and OSCR can use specified information and accounts-preparation powers.
Trustees can be jointly and severally liable for costs of an appointed person preparing outstanding accounts.
doesNotProve: That every late charity will face the same enforcement step.
That lateness alone proves fraud or misuse.

sources.title: Using and updating the register: searching the register, version 2.0
supports: The Northern Ireland register search can export organisation-level search results to CSV.
The Commission says its download excludes UK GDPR and other sensitive data.
doesNotProve: A stable unattended bulk-feed URL or update service level.
Permission to scrape trustee pages or documents into a people database.

sources.title: Annual reporting
supports: Registered Northern Ireland charities must file an annual monitoring return, accounts, trustees' annual report and scrutiny report.
Reporting and accounting detail varies by income and legal structure.
doesNotProve: That every reported figure is comparable or that the regulator certifies an organisation's impact.

sources.title: Companies House data products
supports: Monthly company snapshots, electronic accounts data and PSC snapshots are public data products.
Companies House warns reusers that they remain responsible for data protection, copyright and other law.
doesNotProve: That a company is a charity without an exact charity-regulator link.
That a person with significant control is the beneficial owner of charitable assets.

sources.title: Government grants statistics 2024 to 2025
supports: An annual government grants register publishes scheme and award data with quality notes.
The 2024 to 2025 release has an inherent publication lag.
doesNotProve: A complete live picture of all public and private funding.
That a recipient is a charity without a reliable identifier.

sources.title: Open contracting
supports: Find a Tender and Contracts Finder publish machine-readable contracting notices.
Supplier charity or company identifiers appear only where the buyer supplied them.
doesNotProve: That every award has a usable charity identifier or that a name-only match is correct.
The full performance or payment history of a contract.

sources.title: Special category data: conditions for processing
supports: Religious or philosophical belief is special-category personal data.
Manifestly public requires a deliberate act by the individual and does not remove lawful-basis, fairness, transparency or other duties.
doesNotProve: That regulator publication makes belief inference or bulk republication lawful.
That TaxSorted can inherit a regulator's statutory public-task basis.

sources.title: What is special category data?
supports: Personal data revealing religious or philosophical beliefs is special-category data.
Deliberately assigning an inferred special-category characteristic is special-category processing regardless of whether the inference is correct.
doesNotProve: A lawful basis or Article 9 condition for a proposed TaxSorted people dataset.
That organisational charitable-purpose classifications reveal any person's belief.

regulators.name: Charity Commission for England and Wales

evidence.locator: Parts 4–6 and 8, and Schedule 1
Register data download service

regulators.role: Registers eligible charities within its jurisdiction, maintains the public register, issues guidance and regulates charity trustees' administration of charities.
powers: Determine applications for registration and maintain the statutory register.
Require information, open inquiries and use protective or remedial powers where statutory tests are met.
Set annual-return questions and receive reports and accounts.
limits: It does not recognise charities for HMRC tax relief.
Its register omits lawful unregistered, excepted and exempt charities.
Registration does not certify every activity, claim, contact detail or future use of data.

regulators.name: Office of the Scottish Charity Regulator

evidence.locator: Scottish Charity Register download and API sections
Annual return, accounts and external scrutiny requirements

regulators.role: Keeps the Scottish Charity Register and receives annual regulatory information from registered Scottish charities.
powers: Publish and provide licensed access to Scottish Charity Register data.
Receive annual returns, accounts, trustees' reports and external scrutiny reports.
Mark late annual information on the public register under its reporting process.
limits: It does not decide HMRC tax recognition.
Register data and filed documents remain subject to stated reuse conditions and data-protection law.
A late filing marker is not by itself proof of fraud or misuse.

regulators.name: Charity Commission for Northern Ireland

evidence.locator: Searching and downloading from the register
Annual monitoring return and public-register publication

regulators.role: Maintains the Northern Ireland register of charities and receives annual regulatory information from registered charities.
powers: Provide public register search and organisation-level CSV export.
Receive annual monitoring returns, accounts, trustees' reports and scrutiny reports.
Publish filing status and consider regulatory action for continuing non-compliance.
limits: Its browser export is not documented as a stable unattended bulk-feed service.
CCNI says its CSV excludes information it treats as UK GDPR or sensitive; that does not settle a reuser's field-level personal-data assessment.
It does not grant HMRC recognition for tax relief.

regulators.name: HM Revenue & Customs

evidence.locator: Tax reliefs and when charities pay tax
Conditions and application for tax recognition
Return required when tax is due or HMRC asks

regulators.role: Recognises qualifying bodies as charities for UK tax purposes, administers charity tax reliefs and collects tax where exemptions do not apply.
powers: Administer applications for charity tax recognition.
Administer Gift Aid and other charity relief claims.
Receive charity tax returns when tax is due or a return is requested.
limits: HMRC recognition is not entry on a charity regulator's public register.
Recognition does not make all income, purchases, property or trading tax-free.
Tax details submitted to HMRC are not an open charity directory.

regulators.name: Companies House

evidence.locator: Company, accounts and PSC data products and reuse warning

regulators.role: Maintains public company data and filing products, including records relevant to charitable companies and charity trading subsidiaries.
powers: Publish monthly snapshots of live-company information from the public register.
Publish company, electronic-accounts and people-with-significant-control data products.
Provide company-number-linked public data while warning reusers about their own legal duties.
limits: Company registration does not establish charitable status.
PSC describes statutory company control, not beneficial ownership of restricted charity assets.
Public company data may contain personal data; reusers carry their own legal duties.

regulators.name: Local billing authorities in England

evidence.locator: How to get charitable rate relief

regulators.role: Administer business-rates accounts and charitable and discretionary relief for properties in their local areas.
powers: Request evidence of eligible occupation and charitable use.
Apply mandatory relief and decide discretionary relief under the relevant rules.
Revise bills when property use or circumstances change.
limits: A council cannot turn charity status into automatic relief for every property or use.
Discretionary top-up policy can vary by authority.
This record covers England, not the different rating systems elsewhere in the UK.

regulators.name: Information Commissioner's Office

evidence.locator: Article 9 conditions, public-domain caution and controller duties

regulators.role: Regulates UK data-protection law and publishes guidance relevant to public-register reuse and religious-belief data.
powers: Publish guidance on lawful basis and special-category processing conditions.
Explain fairness, transparency and reasonable-expectation duties for public-domain information.
Explain that a new reuser becomes a controller with its own obligations.
limits: General guidance is not a case-specific clearance for a TaxSorted dataset.
The ICO does not grant charity status or charity tax relief.
A regulator's publication decision does not transfer its lawful basis to a separate controller.

registers.name: Register of Charities for England and Wales: daily extract

evidence.locator: Daily extract description, data definition and listed tables

stableIdentifiers: registered charity number plus linked-charity suffix where applicable
availableOrganisationFields: Registration status, legal and working names, company number and public organisation contact fields.
Purposes, activities, beneficiaries, geography and governing-document metadata.
Annual-return history, income, expenditure, grants, contracts, policies, subsidiaries, pay bands and filed-report links where reported.
excludedOrUnreliable: Lawful unregistered, excepted and exempt charities are not a complete part of this register.
Self-reported activity and contact fields are not regulator warranties.
The trustee table and possible residential public contact addresses are outside TaxSorted v1.
rightsAndConditions: Apply the source terms and attribution at snapshot time.
Open licensing does not license personal-data processing or remove controller duties.
Do not treat the extract as current after its recorded snapshot date.
registers.ingestionReason: V1 links to the official door but publishes no organisation rows; a future field allowlist and privacy assessment must exclude people and unsafe contact data.

registers.name: Scottish Charity Register download and beta API

evidence.locator: Full register, annual-return download, beta API and terms
Publication of accounts as received from 9 March 2026

registers.updateCadence: Full register described as daily; five-year return download available; beta API access requires application.
availableOrganisationFields: Status, legal form, purposes, beneficiaries, activities, geography, public contact and website.
Reported income, expenditure, parent status and designated religious charity indicator.
Five-year annual-return download; accounts and reports submitted on or after 9 March 2026 are published on the register for five years.
excludedOrUnreliable: Filed documents may contain unnecessary personal material because OSCR publishes them in the form received.
A designated religious charity field is an organisation-level statutory status, not evidence of any person's belief.
Income can include subsidiary amounts and needs its published context.
rightsAndConditions: Use the specified Crown copyright and database-right attribution.
Do not use the data for direct marketing.
Do not claim a derived list is the Scottish Charity Register.
registers.ingestionReason: The official page was available but the actual download endpoint was not reliably retrievable in the 2026-07-10 review; never scrape around an unavailable licensed feed.

registers.name: Northern Ireland register of charities CSV export

evidence.locator: Downloads from the register of charities, page 11

registers.updateCadence: No stable unattended bulk-feed cadence was confirmed in the reviewed official guidance.
stableIdentifiers: Northern Ireland charity number in exported register rows
availableOrganisationFields: Organisation identity, status, purposes, activities, public contact and annual-reporting fields included in the 38-field CSV described by the guide.
excludedOrUnreliable: The Commission says the CSV excludes UK GDPR and other sensitive information.
Public address, email and telephone fields still require a field-level personal-data and home-address review before reuse.
The register is not a complete picture of the Northern Ireland sector; current registration and Expression of Intent context must be checked before treating absence as non-charitable status.
Trustee pages and filed documents are not part of the admitted organisation export.
A browser export is not a promised unattended data service.
rightsAndConditions: Confirm reuse terms before storing or redistributing any exported row.
Do not expand the export by scraping people or document pages.
Stop collection if an official export cannot be obtained as documented.
registers.ingestionReason: V1 provides the official search door only; automation waits for a stable supported feed and confirmed reuse terms.

registers.name: Companies House company and accounts data products

evidence.locator: Company, accounts and PSC data products and using company data

registers.updateCadence: Company snapshot monthly; electronic accounts products daily and monthly; consult the current product page for each feed.
availableOrganisationFields: Company identity, status, type, registered office, SIC codes and filing dates.
Electronically filed accounts in iXBRL or XBRL where available.
Separate PSC snapshot data for company-law control.
excludedOrUnreliable: Only electronically filed accounts appear in the structured accounts product.
A company is not proven charitable without an exact regulator identifier link.
PSC and office fields can contain personal data and are outside the v1 charity corpus.
rightsAndConditions: Reusers remain responsible for data protection, copyright and other applicable law.
Join a charity only by an exact filed company number, never by name similarity.
Describe PSC as statutory company control, not ownership of charitable assets.
registers.ingestionReason: Only product metadata and the exact-ID rule are published in v1; no company, officer or PSC rows are copied.

registers.name: Government Grants Register

evidence.locator: 2024 to 2025 scheme and award data and quality information

registers.updateCadence: Annual release, normally after the covered financial year.
stableIdentifiers: recipient charity or company number where supplied
availableOrganisationFields: Grant scheme, award, funder, recipient, value and financial-year fields described by the release.
excludedOrUnreliable: The annual snapshot is not live and is not all public or private funding.
Recipient identity fields vary; a name alone cannot establish a charity match.
Individual-recipient data is outside this corpus.
rightsAndConditions: Preserve release, period, source and quality caveats.
Publish a charity link only where a regulator or company identifier matches exactly.
Do not publish rejected or fuzzy match candidates.
registers.ingestionReason: V1 explains the source but includes no award or recipient records; future admission requires a field audit and exact organisation identifiers.

registers.name: Contracts Finder and Find a Tender open-contracting outputs

evidence.locator: OCDS outputs, supplier identifiers and API links

registers.updateCadence: Notice-driven APIs and dated outputs; source page freshness and current API documentation must be checked before collection.
stableIdentifiers: supplier company or charity number where the buyer supplied one
availableOrganisationFields: Buyer, supplier, procurement stage, award, contract and notice fields in official OCDS outputs.
excludedOrUnreliable: Supplier organisation identifiers are optional and may be absent.
A contract notice is not proof of delivery, outcome or all payments.
Notice contact fields and name-only supplier matches are outside v1.
rightsAndConditions: Preserve the OCID, notice identifier, publication date and source URL.
Join to a charity only through an exact published charity or company number.
Quarantine records without a reliable organisation identifier.
registers.ingestionReason: No notices or suppliers are included in v1; the official source page is dated and needs an endpoint-level review before future ingestion.

legalForms.name: Charitable company limited by guarantee

evidence.locator: Charitable company section
Charitable company and incorporated-structure sections
Comparison with charitable companies reporting to OSCR and Companies House

legalForms.governingDocument: Articles of association; the company is limited by guarantee rather than shares when using the common charity form.
legalForms.governance: Charity trustees are normally also company directors and must comply with the duties attached to both roles.
legalForms.membership: The articles define members and their voting rights; a guarantee is not an equity share.
legalForms.assetHolding: The company can hold property, employ staff and enter contracts in its own name.
legalForms.ownerModel: There is no shareholder equity owner in the common limited-by-guarantee charity structure; assets remain committed to the charitable purposes and governing document.
legalForms.reportingShape: A charitable company reports to Companies House and to the charity regulator or regulators that apply to it.
notEquivalentTo: A company limited by shares with distributable equity.
A trading subsidiary merely because both are companies.
Proof that every company filing is current or complete.

legalForms.name: Charitable incorporated organisation

evidence.locator: Charitable incorporated organisation section
CIO foundation and association structures

legalForms.governingDocument: A CIO constitution, using an association or foundation governance model.
legalForms.governance: Trustees govern the CIO; an association CIO also has a wider voting membership, while a foundation CIO's voting members are its trustees.
legalForms.membership: Membership follows the constitution and gives governance rights, not beneficial ownership of charity assets.
legalForms.assetHolding: The CIO can own property, employ staff and contract in its own name.
legalForms.ownerModel: The CIO is the legal person holding its assets for its charitable purposes; trustees and members do not receive equity ownership.
legalForms.reportingShape: The CIO registers and files with the Charity Commission rather than Companies House.
notEquivalentTo: A company registration at Companies House.
A route for distributing residual assets to members.
Automatic HMRC recognition for tax relief.

legalForms.name: Scottish charitable incorporated organisation

evidence.locator: SCIO background, legal framework and key facts

legalForms.governingDocument: A SCIO constitution containing its charitable purposes and governance rules.
legalForms.governance: The SCIO acts through its members and charity trustees under its constitution and Scottish charity law.
legalForms.membership: A SCIO has members under a single-tier or two-tier constitutional structure; membership is a governance relationship.
legalForms.assetHolding: The SCIO can transact, employ, incur debts and hold property in its own name.
legalForms.ownerModel: The corporate body holds the assets for its charitable purposes; members and trustees are not equity owners.
legalForms.reportingShape: OSCR is the single corporate and charity regulator for the SCIO form.
notEquivalentTo: A charitable company reporting to Companies House.
Personal ownership by a trustee or member.
A guarantee that trustees can never incur personal liability.

legalForms.name: Charitable trust

evidence.locator: Charitable trust section
Trust and unincorporated-structure sections

legalForms.governingDocument: A trust deed, will or other trust instrument sets the charitable purposes and trustees' powers.
legalForms.governance: Trustees administer the trust property under the trust instrument, charity law and fiduciary duties.
legalForms.membership: A trust normally has no equity shareholders or voting membership body.
legalForms.assetHolding: Trustees or holding trustees hold legal title to assets on trust and may have to contract in their own names for the charity.
legalForms.ownerModel: Legal title does not mean the trustees can use trust assets as their own; the assets are restricted to the charitable trust and its purposes.
legalForms.reportingShape: Where registration and reporting rules require, the applicable charity regulator receives reporting; contracts and property need careful handling because the trust lacks separate legal personality.
notEquivalentTo: A trustee's beneficial personal property.
A body with separate legal personality.
An incorporated charity with limited liability.

legalForms.name: Unincorporated charitable association

evidence.locator: Unincorporated charitable association section
Unincorporated association and membership sections

legalForms.governingDocument: A constitution or rules set the purposes, membership and committee powers.
legalForms.governance: A committee of charity trustees governs under the constitution, often with a wider voting membership.
legalForms.membership: Members may elect trustees or decide reserved matters but do not receive equity in charitable assets.
legalForms.assetHolding: Because the association is not a separate person, trustees or holding trustees enter contracts and hold property for it.
legalForms.ownerModel: The association has no equity owner; legal holders and members remain bound by the charitable purposes and governing rules.
legalForms.reportingShape: Where registration and reporting rules require, the applicable charity regulator receives reporting; contracts and property need careful handling because the association lacks legal personality.
notEquivalentTo: A CIO or company capable of contracting in its own name.
A members' club whose assets can be distributed privately.
Personal beneficial ownership by office holders.

taxTreatments.name: HMRC recognition is a separate tax gateway

evidence.locator: Conditions for recognition and online application
Recognition requirement, qualifying relief and taxable income boundary

taxTreatments.eligibility: A UK-based body established for charitable purposes only, registered with an applicable charity regulator if that applies to it, and run by fit and proper persons, subject to HMRC's full conditions.
taxTreatments.benefit: Recognition opens access to the charity tax relief and reclaim processes for which the body qualifies.
conditions: Apply through HMRC's official service and provide the information it requests.
Register with an applicable charity regulator first where registration is required.
Continue to meet the tax-law charity conditions.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Before recognition or where a specific relief's conditions are not met.
Where income or gains are taxable or are used outside charitable purposes.
Where HMRC requires a return or adjusts a claim under the applicable rules.
taxTreatments.reasoning: HMRC says recognition is required to benefit from charity tax reliefs, and registration with a charity regulator is one condition where it applies.
notEquivalentTo: Entry on a charity regulator's register.
Endorsement of an organisation or belief.
A blanket exemption from VAT or every other tax.

taxTreatments.name: Income and gains relief is conditional on charitable application

evidence.locator: Sections 1–6: main exemptions, land, interest, trading and capital gains

taxTreatments.eligibility: A body recognised as a charity for tax purposes receiving a class of income or gain covered by the statutory charity exemptions.
taxTreatments.benefit: Qualifying donations, investment and rental income, primary-purpose trading profits and capital gains can be exempt from Income Tax or Corporation Tax.
conditions: The relevant statutory exemption must cover the receipt or gain.
Income and gains must be applied to charitable purposes under the tax rules.
Investments and property transactions must stay within their specific conditions.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Property development or dealing can be taxable trading rather than exempt investment or disposal.
A receipt outside an exemption remains taxable.
Non-charitable expenditure can restrict otherwise attributable exempt income and gains.
taxTreatments.reasoning: The relief follows qualifying sources and charitable application, not the organisation's label alone.
notEquivalentTo: No tax return ever being required.
All property or investment activity being exempt.
Permission to distribute profits privately.

taxTreatments.name: Non-charitable expenditure restricts relief

evidence.locator: Attributable income and gains and restriction to relief

taxTreatments.eligibility: Applies where a charity incurs expenditure that is not charitable expenditure under the tax rules and has attributable income or gains.
taxTreatments.benefit: No additional benefit; this rule limits the exemptions to preserve their charitable-purpose condition.
conditions: Identify the non-charitable expenditure and the attributable income and gains.
Apply HMRC's allocation and notification rules.
File the required return and account for resulting tax.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Relief is disallowed on an amount of attributable income or gains under the restriction.
A return and payment can be required even though the body remains a charity.
Separate anti-avoidance or taxable-source rules may also apply.
taxTreatments.reasoning: The restriction connects the favourable tax treatment to actual charitable application of resources.
notEquivalentTo: Automatic removal from the charity register.
Proof of dishonesty by a trustee.
A rule that every administrative cost is non-charitable.

taxTreatments.name: Charity trading relief has purpose and scale boundaries

evidence.locator: Primary purpose, ancillary and small trading exemption sections

taxTreatments.eligibility: Trading that directly carries out a charitable purpose, is ancillary to it, is mainly carried out by beneficiaries, or falls within the small non-primary-purpose trading exemption.
taxTreatments.benefit: Qualifying trading profits can be exempt from Income Tax or Corporation Tax when applied to the charity's purposes.
conditions: Classify each trade by what it actually does, not its fundraising label.
Keep non-primary-purpose turnover within the current statutory small-trading limit if relying on that exemption.
Apply qualifying profits solely to the charity's purposes.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Non-primary-purpose trading above the exemption boundary can make profits taxable.
A mixed trade may require apportionment.
VAT, PAYE and other taxes remain separate questions.
taxTreatments.reasoning: Primary-purpose and qualifying small trading profits may be relieved; other trading profits are taxable unless another rule applies.
notEquivalentTo: Every sale by a charity being tax-free.
A VAT exemption.
A licence to place commercial risk on protected charity assets.

taxTreatments.name: A trading subsidiary separates risk but is ordinarily taxable

evidence.locator: Using a subsidiary trading company and donations to parent charity

taxTreatments.eligibility: A separately incorporated company owned or controlled by a charity and used for non-primary-purpose or risk-bearing trade.
taxTreatments.benefit: The separate company can ring-fence trading risk and may deduct a qualifying charitable donation made to its parent within the applicable time and tax rules.
conditions: The subsidiary is a genuine separate company with its own directors, accounts and tax obligations.
Any charity investment in it must be justifiable and managed in the charity's interests.
Donations and VAT treatment follow their specific rules and deadlines.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Undonated profits and ordinary taxable supplies remain subject to company tax and VAT rules.
A late or non-qualifying donation may not produce the intended Corporation Tax deduction.
An imprudent charity investment can be non-charitable expenditure.
taxTreatments.reasoning: The structure manages commercial risk; it does not make a commercial company itself tax-exempt.
notEquivalentTo: The parent charity.
A tax-free shell.
Evidence that subsidiary and parent figures are always reported separately in public summaries.

taxTreatments.name: Gift Aid adds tax to eligible individual donations

evidence.locator: Eligible and ineligible claims

taxTreatments.eligibility: An eligible donation from an individual to a charity or CASC recognised by HMRC, supported by a valid Gift Aid declaration and the donor-tax conditions.
taxTreatments.benefit: The charity can reclaim basic-rate tax under Gift Aid for a qualifying donation.
conditions: The donor makes a valid declaration and has paid sufficient qualifying tax.
The payment is a donation rather than an excluded purchase, company donation or excessive-benefit arrangement.
The charity keeps the required records and makes a valid claim.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: HMRC can reject or recover an invalid claim.
A donor may have to cover a shortfall where insufficient tax was paid.
Payments outside the qualifying rules receive no Gift Aid uplift.
taxTreatments.reasoning: Gift Aid redirects qualifying basic-rate tax to support the donation; it is not a general subsidy for every receipt.
notEquivalentTo: Publication of donor names or individual donations.
Every donation being worth 25% more.
Charity-regulator registration alone.

taxTreatments.name: Charities have no blanket VAT exemption

evidence.locator: Charities and VAT registration, taxable turnover and exempt supplies

taxTreatments.eligibility: Specific charitable goods, services, purchases or fundraising events may qualify for a zero rate, reduced rate or exemption under the VAT rules.
taxTreatments.benefit: Only the specific supply or purchase receives the VAT treatment for which all conditions are met.
conditions: Classify each business and non-business activity and each supply under VAT rules.
Register when VAT-taxable turnover exceeds the current registration threshold, subject to the full rules.
Apply input-tax recovery restrictions, including those linked to exempt activity.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Taxable supplies remain subject to VAT even when made by a charity.
Input VAT may be irrecoverable on exempt or non-business activities.
Incorrect relief or registration treatment can create assessments, interest or penalties.
taxTreatments.reasoning: VAT follows the character of supplies and purchases; charitable status changes particular rules but does not erase the tax.
notEquivalentTo: Exemption from registering for VAT.
All purchases being zero-rated.
Full recovery of input VAT.

taxTreatments.name: Charitable business-rates relief in England

evidence.locator: Eligibility, reduction, council application and change reporting

taxTreatments.eligibility: A property occupied and used wholly or mainly for charitable purposes under the applicable English rating rules, subject to the current private-school exclusion and the full eligibility rules.
taxTreatments.benefit: Mandatory charitable relief can reduce the business-rates bill by up to 80%; the local authority may add discretionary relief.
conditions: Apply to the local billing authority and provide evidence requested.
Meet the occupation and charitable-use conditions for the property.
Report relevant changes in use or property circumstances.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: The remaining bill is payable unless discretionary or another relief applies.
Ineligible use or changed circumstances can reduce relief and produce a revised bill.
Discretionary relief can differ between councils.
taxTreatments.reasoning: The mandatory reduction is conditional on qualifying occupation and charitable use; the local council administers the bill and decides whether any discretionary relief applies.
notEquivalentTo: Automatic zero business rates.
Relief for an excluded private school merely because it is a charity.
The rating rules in Scotland, Wales or Northern Ireland.
Proof that every property held by a charity is used charitably.

taxTreatments.name: Charity relief from Stamp Duty Land Tax

evidence.locator: General overview, conditions and withdrawal links

taxTreatments.eligibility: A qualifying charity or charitable trust acquiring land and meeting the statutory charitable-use and other conditions.
taxTreatments.benefit: The qualifying land transaction can receive SDLT relief.
conditions: Claim the relief on the transaction return where required.
Hold and use the land within the qualifying charitable-purpose conditions.
Apply the specific joint-purchase, trust and anti-avoidance rules.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: A disqualifying event while the charity still owns the land within the statutory period can withdraw relief.
Non-qualifying use or purchaser arrangements can prevent relief.
Different land-transaction taxes apply in Scotland and Wales.
taxTreatments.reasoning: Relief depends on qualifying charitable holding and use, and the cited disqualifying events can withdraw it.
notEquivalentTo: Every property purchase being tax-free.
Relief from Land and Buildings Transaction Tax or Land Transaction Tax.
Permanent relief regardless of later use.

taxTreatments.name: TaxSorted analysis: the public-benefit tax bargain

evidence.locator: Public benefit and no automatic presumption
Charitable-purpose condition across main exemptions
Restriction when resources are used non-charitably

taxTreatments.eligibility: TaxSorted analysis of the relationship between public-benefit charity law and conditional tax rules; it is not a separate relief or legal test.
taxTreatments.benefit: The combined framework lets qualifying resources remain available for charitable purposes rather than being taxed in the ordinary way.
conditions: The organisation must satisfy the applicable charity and public-benefit law.
It must satisfy HMRC's tax-recognition and each relief's statutory conditions.
Assets, income and gains remain directed to charitable purposes rather than private equity return.
whenTaxOrClawbackCanArise: Income and gains outside the applicable exemption conditions remain taxable.
Non-charitable expenditure can restrict relief.
taxTreatments.reasoning: TaxSorted analysis: the coherent policy bargain is public benefit plus locked charitable application in exchange for conditional relief. This is an inference from the linked framework, not a statutory quotation or claim that government endorses a religion.
notEquivalentTo: Automatic relief because an organisation is religious.
Government approval of a belief or doctrine.
Proof that charity status alone satisfies a particular tax exemption's conditions.

obligations.name: Purposes and operation must be charitable for public benefit

evidence.locator: Sections 1–4: charity, charitable purposes and public benefit
No automatic presumption and advancement of religion section
Purpose, operation and trustees' annual report duties

obligations.appliesTo: Every body claiming charity status under England and Wales charity law, including one whose purposes include advancement of religion.
obligations.trigger: Claiming or retaining charity status and carrying out the organisation's purposes.
requirement: Have only purposes that fall within charity law and are for the public benefit.
Carry out those purposes for public benefit rather than private benefit beyond what the law permits.
Trustees consider and report public benefit under the applicable rules.
obligations.deadline: Continuous; public-benefit reporting forms part of the relevant annual-report cycle.
nonCompliance: A purpose label alone cannot secure registration or prove continuing charity status.
The Commission can examine whether an organisation meets and operates within charity law.
Tax relief remains a separate HMRC question.
obligations.practicalCheck: Read the governing purposes and latest trustees' report; do not infer a person's belief or assume public benefit from a religion classification.

obligations.name: Registration, excepted and exempt boundaries in England and Wales

evidence.locator: What excepted charities are, duration and church charities
Part 4 registration framework

obligations.appliesTo: Charities within the statutory registration rules, subject to income thresholds and specific excepted or exempt categories.
obligations.trigger: Meeting the registration conditions or ceasing to fall within a registration exception.
requirement: Apply to register where required and keep the register information current.
Excepted charities remain subject to charity law even though they do not register or submit annual returns.
Do not confuse an excepted charity with an exempt charity.
obligations.deadline: The time-limited exception for qualifying linked church charities with income of £100,000 or less is currently stated to end on 31 March 2031; recheck current law before that date.
nonCompliance: A charity required to register cannot rely on absence from the register as permission to ignore charity law.
The Commission can regulate an excepted charity despite its absence from the register.
Failure to register when required can obstruct reporting, funding checks and tax-recognition processes.
obligations.practicalCheck: Absence from the register is not proof that a body is not charitable; check the current threshold, excepted and exempt rules.

obligations.name: Annual reporting in England and Wales

evidence.locator: Deadline and current income/structure reporting bands
Expected 30 September 2026 threshold changes and SORP 2026

obligations.appliesTo: Registered charities in England and Wales, with questions and document requirements depending on income and legal form.
requirement: Under the current reviewed bands, report income and expenditure at £10,000 or less and complete an annual return above £10,000.

Under the current reviewed bands, income above £25,000 requires the annual return plus accounts, trustees' annual report and scrutiny report; CIOs file accounts and a trustees' report regardless of income.
Use the accounting and audit rules applying to the charity's period and structure.
obligations.deadline: Within 10 months after the financial year end. Announced accounting and examination threshold changes are expected for accounting years ending on or after 30 September 2026, so current guidance must be rechecked at transition.
nonCompliance: The public register can show reporting as overdue.
Missing information reduces the evidence available to donors, funders, beneficiaries and the regulator.
The regulator can use its statutory information and regulatory processes where appropriate.
obligations.practicalCheck: Record the financial year end and rule version; never apply a threshold solely from an old snapshot.

obligations.name: Annual reporting in Scotland

evidence.locator: Annual return, documents, nine-month deadline and accounting form
Publication of submitted accounts from 9 March 2026
Defaulting, section 29 and section 45 consequences

obligations.appliesTo: Every charity registered in Scotland, including cross-border charities.
obligations.trigger: Each financial year after entry in the Scottish Charity Register.
requirement: Submit the online annual return, annual accounts, trustees' annual report and external scrutiny report.
Use receipts-and-payments or fully accrued accounts only where the legal form, income, governing document and other requirements allow.
Prepare filed documents knowing OSCR publishes submitted reports and accounts in the form received.
obligations.deadline: Within nine months after the financial year end; cross-border charities must meet the Scottish deadline even where another regulator allows longer.
nonCompliance: OSCR marks the charity as defaulting and the delay is visible.
Failure is a breach of trustee duties and can lead to information requirements or an appointed person preparing accounts at trustees' cost under the stated process.
Continued non-filing and non-engagement can lead OSCR to remove a defaulting charity from the Scottish Charity Register under the current process.
obligations.practicalCheck: Remove unnecessary personal material and handwritten signatures from accounts before filing; public filing does not make extraction into a people database safe.

obligations.name: Annual reporting in Northern Ireland

evidence.locator: Annual monitoring return, accounts, scrutiny and public status

requirement: Submit the online annual monitoring return.
Submit annual accounts, trustees' annual report and an independent examination or audit report as applicable.
Use the accounting form and scrutiny level required by income and legal structure.
obligations.deadline: Within 10 months after the financial year end under the current Commission reporting process.
nonCompliance: The register can mark accounts and reports late or outstanding.
The Commission can contact trustees and consider further regulatory action for unresolved default.
Late status can affect funder due diligence without proving any wider wrongdoing.
obligations.practicalCheck: Use the current CCNI guidance and the charity's own register page; the browser export is not a substitute for filed reports.

obligations.name: File a charity tax return when tax is due or HMRC asks

evidence.locator: When to file, return type, deadlines and penalties
Return and tax liability after non-charitable expenditure restriction

obligations.appliesTo: A recognised charity with tax to pay, non-qualifying income or expenditure, or a notice from HMRC requiring a return.
obligations.trigger: Tax becomes due or HMRC asks the charity to file, even if the charity expects no tax to be payable.
requirement: Use the return appropriate to a charitable trust or other charity body.
Include the charity supplementary information and accounts required by the filing route.
Calculate and pay tax arising after exemptions and restrictions.
obligations.deadline: Depends on the return type and accounting or tax period; use the date in the HMRC notice and current filing guidance.
nonCompliance: Late or missing returns can attract the ordinary return consequences, including penalties under the applicable rules.
Tax, interest or adjusted relief can remain payable even though the body is still a charity.
A regulator return does not replace an HMRC return.
obligations.practicalCheck: Separate regulator filing status from HMRC tax status; neither public register can show the full confidential tax account.

obligations.name: A public-data reuser becomes its own controller

evidence.locator: Manifestly made public, controller obligations and transparency
Religious-belief data and deliberate inferences

obligations.appliesTo: TaxSorted or any other service processing personal data taken from public charity, company, account or funding sources.
obligations.trigger: Collecting, linking, inferring, storing, searching, publishing or otherwise processing information about an identifiable person.
requirement: Identify and document an Article 6 lawful basis and ensure the processing is lawful, fair and transparent.
For special-category belief data, identify and document an Article 9 condition as well as the Article 6 basis.
Tell affected individuals about the processing as the applicable transparency rules require; do not assume manifestly public merely because a regulator or charity published information.
obligations.deadline: Identify the lawful basis and any Article 9 condition before processing, and provide transparency information as the applicable law requires.
nonCompliance: Open licensing does not cure unlawful personal-data processing.
A mistaken belief inference can still be special-category processing.
Public-domain availability does not remove fairness and transparency duties.
obligations.practicalCheck: V1 excludes people, personal contacts, named pay and inferred beliefs; any later people layer requires a separate necessity, lawful-basis and special-category assessment before code or collection.

fundingMechanisms.name: Donations and Gift Aid

evidence.locator: Gift Aid eligibility and exclusions
Interest, Gift Aid donations and charitable-purpose condition
Annual-return and published-report organisation-level finance fields

fundingMechanisms.flow: A person or organisation gives money or assets without buying equivalent goods or services; eligible individual cash donations may carry a Gift Aid reclaim.
publicEvidence: In England and Wales, annual returns and published accounts expose aggregate donation and fundraising information where the reporting question applies.
The evidence is organisation-level rather than an open donor schedule.
limits: Not every receipt called a donation qualifies for Gift Aid.
Donor declarations and individual donation schedules are not public-sector transparency data.
Restrictions imposed by a donor can affect how funds may be used.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Publish aggregate organisation-level totals and source periods only; never expose or infer individual donors or donations.

fundingMechanisms.name: Public and private grants

evidence.locator: Annual government scheme and award data with quality notes
Annual-return grant-income fields and filed reports
Main charity income exemptions and charitable-purpose condition

fundingMechanisms.flow: A government body, foundation, charity or other funder awards money for stated purposes, outputs or costs under grant conditions.
publicEvidence: Government Grants Register scheme and award rows where published.

In England and Wales, charity annual-return and filed-account grant categories, notes and restricted-fund disclosures where applicable; equivalent evidence elsewhere needs the territorial filing source.
limits: The government register is annual and does not cover all funders.
A grant award does not prove all money was paid, spent or effective.
Recipient names without stable identifiers cannot support a published join.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Join an award to a charity only by an exact official identifier and preserve funder, award period, restrictions and data-quality notes.

fundingMechanisms.name: Public-service and other contracts

evidence.locator: OCDS notices and optional supplier organisation identifiers
Annual-return government-contract fields
Trading and subsidiary tax boundaries
VAT follows taxable supplies and registration rules

fundingMechanisms.flow: A charity or its subsidiary supplies specified goods or services in exchange for payment under a contract rather than receiving a gift.
publicEvidence: Find a Tender or Contracts Finder notices and OCDS identifiers where the procurement is published.

In England and Wales, organisation annual returns and accounts can distinguish government contract income where reporting questions apply; equivalent evidence elsewhere needs the territorial filing source.
limits: Notice data can omit supplier charity or company numbers.
Award value is not automatically cash received or the charity's unrestricted surplus.
Contract income can be trading and VAT treatment depends on the supply.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Keep grant and contract flows distinct, use exact supplier identifiers, and never claim delivery outcomes from an award notice alone.

fundingMechanisms.name: Primary-purpose and ancillary trading

evidence.locator: Primary-purpose, ancillary and beneficiary trading
England and Wales annual-return and published-report fields

fundingMechanisms.flow: The charity charges for goods or services that directly carry out or support its stated charitable purposes.
publicEvidence: In England and Wales, filed accounts and reports can describe trading and charitable-activity income, accounting policy, purposes and activities.
Equivalent evidence elsewhere in the UK must come from the applicable territorial filing source.
limits: The accounting label does not decide the tax classification.
Mixed or public-facing activity can require apportionment.
VAT and employment taxes remain separate.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Describe the organisation's own reported activity as self-reported and keep the tax classification conditional.

fundingMechanisms.name: Non-primary-purpose trading and subsidiaries

evidence.locator: Small trading exemption and subsidiary company sections
England and Wales subsidiary and annual-return fields
UK company-number and accounts products

fundingMechanisms.flow: Commercial activity raises funds but does not itself carry out the charity's purposes; smaller activity may fit the statutory exemption and material activity may be placed in a subsidiary.
publicEvidence: In England and Wales, charity returns and reports can disclose subsidiaries and trading information.
Across the UK, exact company-number links and separate electronic subsidiary accounts may be available at Companies House.
limits: A subsidiary is ordinarily taxable and must be treated as a separate entity.
Parent and group totals can be confused if consolidation is not stated.
Charity investment in a subsidiary must be prudent and in the charity's interests.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Publish exact parent-subsidiary relationships only when a filed identifier or account note supports them; never infer a corporate group by name.

fundingMechanisms.name: Investment and property income

evidence.locator: Property income, interest, investments and capital gains
Published reports and higher-income balance-sheet fields

fundingMechanisms.flow: A charity holds cash, investments or property to produce income or longer-term resources for its charitable purposes.
publicEvidence: In England and Wales, higher-income return fields and published reports can show balance-sheet asset classes, investment information and fund context.
Equivalent Scottish or Northern Ireland evidence needs its own territorial filing source and definitions.
limits: A balance-sheet amount is not a list of each asset or its current market value.
Property development or dealing can be taxable trade.
Group and charity-only asset figures must not be mixed.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Publish accounting-period totals with valuation basis and fund context; do not convert legal title into a claim of trustee beneficial ownership.

fundingMechanisms.name: Fundraising events and lotteries

evidence.locator: Fundraising events and lotteries section
England and Wales annual-return and published-report fundraising fields

fundingMechanisms.flow: Events, raffles or lotteries raise money through participation or sales under charity, tax, VAT and gambling rules.
publicEvidence: In England and Wales, aggregate fundraising income, costs, policies and return answers can appear where the reporting questions apply.
Equivalent evidence elsewhere needs the applicable territorial filing source.
limits: Not every event receives the fundraising-event tax or VAT treatment.
Lottery permission and enforcement are jurisdiction-specific; the cited Great Britain route does not describe Northern Ireland's separate rules.
Gross proceeds do not show net funds raised.
fundingMechanisms.safeUse: Keep gross income, direct costs and net contribution distinct and link readers to current licensing guidance rather than asserting permission.

financeDisclosures.name: England and Wales annual-return income and spending

evidence.locator: Charity and annual-return history, Part A and Part B tables
Income bands and document requirements

financeDisclosures.coverage: Annual headline income and expenditure for registered charities, with more return questions as income and structure require.
whatPublicCanSee: Headline annual income, expenditure, financial year end and filing status.
For charities asked the relevant questions, categories such as donations, trading, grants, contracts and fundraising.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: A return is organisation-reported for a defined period; gross income is not profit, cash or unrestricted money, and late data can describe an older year.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: Publish organisation totals and periods only; do not expose return-user accounts, donor schedules or personal submission details.

financeDisclosures.name: England and Wales filed accounts and balance-sheet context

evidence.locator: Published-report and higher-income annual-return finance fields
Accounts filing requirements by income and CIO status

financeDisclosures.coverage: Filed accounts where the charity's legal form and income require publication, plus structured balance-sheet fields for relevant annual returns.
whatPublicCanSee: Accounting totals for fixed and current assets, liabilities and funds where disclosed.
Notes can describe investments, land, restricted funds, endowments, pensions and commitments.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: Accounts are period-end statements using accounting policies; they are not a live inventory, and consolidated figures may include subsidiaries.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: Link to filed documents rather than bulk-extracting names, signatures, bank details or incidental personal material.

financeDisclosures.name: England and Wales aggregate employee pay bands

evidence.locator: Annual-return employee-benefit band fields and data definition

financeDisclosures.coverage: Organisation-level counts of employees whose benefits fall in reportable bands where annual-return questions apply.
whatPublicCanSee: Counts in published benefit bands beginning at the reporting threshold.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: A band is not a named salary, take-home pay or like-for-like role comparison; benefits, part years, pension and group scope affect meaning.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: No employee names, role-to-band linking, biographies, work histories or attempts to identify a person from a small count.

financeDisclosures.name: England and Wales trustee remuneration indicators

evidence.locator: Annual-return trustee-payment questions and published-report table

financeDisclosures.coverage: Organisation-level return answers and account notes concerning trustee remuneration, benefits or expenses where required.
whatPublicCanSee: Whether the charity reported trustee remuneration or benefits under the applicable return questions.
Filed-account notes may give totals, reasons, authority and number of trustees involved.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: Expenses reimbursed, payment for a separate service and remuneration for acting as trustee are different categories and must not be collapsed.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: V1 publishes definitions only; it does not copy names, named payments or attempt to identify recipients from notes.

financeDisclosures.name: Subsidiaries and group boundaries

evidence.locator: Subsidiary and group fields in annual-return data
Company and electronic-accounts products
Trading subsidiary and charity investment sections

financeDisclosures.coverage: England and Wales filed charity returns and accounts, plus exact company-number links, can reveal trading subsidiaries and group reporting; Companies House remains a UK cross-source rather than proof of charity status.
whatPublicCanSee: Reported subsidiary existence, company number and consolidated or charity-only figures where filed.
Separate company status and electronic accounts where available.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: Control of a subsidiary does not make its gross turnover the parent's unrestricted income; group and entity-only figures need explicit labels.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: Only exact organisation relationships are admissible; officer, PSC and related-person profiles remain excluded.

financeDisclosures.name: Scotland annual returns and filed accounts

evidence.locator: Required annual documents and accounting forms
Five-year publication in the format received from 9 March 2026

financeDisclosures.coverage: Every registered Scottish charity submits accounts, a trustees' annual report and external scrutiny; documents submitted after the 2026 change are published for five years.
whatPublicCanSee: Annual-return financial fields and filing status.
The submitted accounts, trustees' report and scrutiny report on the register.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: Receipts-and-payments and accrued accounts are different; periods, basis, charity/group scope and qualifications must stay attached.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: Because documents are published as received, TaxSorted links rather than mirrors them and does not extract trustee names, signatures or incidental people.

financeDisclosures.name: Northern Ireland annual monitoring and filed accounts

evidence.locator: Annual monitoring, accounting and scrutiny requirements
Register CSV fields and sensitive-data restriction

financeDisclosures.coverage: Registered charities submit an annual monitoring return, accounts, trustees' report and scrutiny report, with detail varying by income and legal form.
whatPublicCanSee: Organisation-level annual-return and filing-status fields in the public register export.
Published accounts and reports through the charity's register entry where available.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: The register is not the whole Northern Ireland sector and the browser CSV is not a stable bulk-feed promise.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: Use only the admitted organisation export after field review; do not scrape trustee pages or copy personal material from documents.

financeDisclosures.name: Government grant and contract award disclosures

evidence.locator: Annual scheme and award data and quality information
Contract notice OCDS outputs and supplier identifiers

financeDisclosures.coverage: Published government grant awards and procurement notices where official releases include the relevant fields.
whatPublicCanSee: Funder or buyer, scheme or procurement, recipient or supplier, date and value fields.
Stable award, notice, OCID, company or charity identifiers where supplied.
financeDisclosures.interpretation: Award value is not necessarily payment, spend, unrestricted income, profit, output or impact; grants and contracts are different flows.
financeDisclosures.privacyBoundary: V1 includes no recipient rows; a future layer must exclude individuals and publish organisation links only through exact official identifiers.

controlModels.name: Trustee stewardship, not equity ownership

evidence.locator: Exclusively charitable purposes and public-benefit requirement
Who runs each structure and how property and contracts are held
Charitable-purpose application condition for tax exemptions

controlModels.decisionRights: Trustees collectively direct the charity within its governing document and for its stated charitable purposes.
controlModels.assetPosition: The structure or legal holders control and apply assets for the charity; trustees cannot treat the assets as private distributable wealth.
accountability: The governing document defines powers and reserved decisions.
Regulator reporting and accounts show organisation-level stewardship.
HMRC relief depends on charitable application under separate tax rules.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: Calling trustees 'owners' collapses governance power, legal title and beneficial ownership into one false idea.

controlModels.name: The incorporated charity is the legal asset holder

evidence.locator: Corporate structures, property, contracts and membership
SCIO as a corporate legal entity

controlModels.decisionRights: Trustees act as the corporate body's governing organ, subject to member decisions reserved by the constitution or articles.
controlModels.assetPosition: The incorporated charity owns property and enters contracts in its own legal name while the charitable purposes restrict use and distribution.
accountability: The charity regulator receives charity reporting.
A charitable company also has company-law filings; a CIO or SCIO uses its charity-law corporate regime.
Members can have votes without equity shares.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: A member or trustee is not the beneficial owner merely because the entity acts through them.

controlModels.name: Trustees hold legal title subject to the trust

evidence.locator: Unincorporated structures, trustees, property and contracts

controlModels.decisionRights: Trustees exercise powers given by the trust deed or constitution and must do so for the charity, not for themselves.
controlModels.assetPosition: Named trustees, holding trustees or a custodian may hold legal title because the unincorporated charity is not a separate person.
accountability: The governing document limits how assets can be used.
Changes of trustee may require title or contractual updates.
The legal holder and the charitable purpose restriction must both be stated when explaining an asset.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: A land title in a trustee's name is not evidence that the land is their beneficial personal asset.

controlModels.name: Membership can carry votes without carrying equity

evidence.locator: Association CIO, company and unincorporated membership structures
SCIO members and corporate form

controlModels.decisionRights: A constitution or articles can let members elect trustees and decide specified constitutional or winding-up matters.
controlModels.assetPosition: Membership does not create a transferable share in charitable assets or a right to private residual distribution.
accountability: Member and trustee powers must be read from the governing document.
A single-tier body can have the same people as members and trustees but the legal capacities remain distinct.
A two-tier body gives a wider membership specified decision rights.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: Voting power is governance, not proof of ownership in the commercial equity sense.

controlModels.name: Parent control of a trading subsidiary

evidence.locator: Using and investing in a subsidiary trading company
Company and accounts data products

controlModels.decisionRights: A charity can set up a separate subsidiary company to carry on trading on its behalf, subject to the company's own governance.
controlModels.assetPosition: The charity's investment in the separate company must be justified, monitored and managed for the charity; the company remains ordinarily taxable on its own activity.
accountability: The charity must justify and monitor its investment.
The subsidiary pays company tax and VAT under the ordinary rules except where a specific relief applies.
Any future public relationship needs an exact filed company number rather than a name match.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: A controlled subsidiary is not the same legal entity or tax account as its parent charity.

controlModels.name: People with significant control is a company-law concept

evidence.locator: PSC snapshot product and reuse responsibilities

controlModels.decisionRights: The Companies House product is a snapshot of PSC information provided to the registrar; v1 does not interpret or republish the individual conditions.
controlModels.assetPosition: A PSC snapshot is a company-control dataset and is not evidence that a person beneficially owns restricted charitable assets.
accountability: Use the company number, snapshot date and source caveats.
Keep charity governance, company control and asset ownership as separate concepts.
Do not republish PSC people in this sector-map v1 corpus, which has no charity-by-charity subject rows.
controlModels.misleadingLabel: Calling every PSC an 'owner of the charity' is legally and conceptually unsafe.

helpRoutes.name: Find an England and Wales charity through the official register

evidence.locator: Register search and full-download service
Excepted and exempt coverage boundary

helpRoutes.purpose: Verify a registered charity's identity, stated activities, status, reporting and own public website before deciding whether to approach it.
helpRoutes.firstStep: Search by charity number where known; otherwise confirm name, place and official number together and read the latest register entry.
askFor: Its current official website and organisation-level contact route.
Its stated purposes, activities, geography and who it says it helps.
Its latest filed report, accounts and whether reporting is current.
safeContactRule: Absence is not conclusive because unregistered, excepted and exempt charities exist.
Do not copy trustee names or a possible residential register address into an outreach list.
Use the charity's current generic service or contact page rather than a person's details.

helpRoutes.name: Find a Scottish charity through the official register

evidence.locator: Scottish Charity Register access and reuse terms
Publication of accounts as received

helpRoutes.purpose: Verify the SC number, status, stated purposes, activities, public contact and filed information through OSCR.
helpRoutes.firstStep: Search by SC number where known and use the charity's own linked website for current service information.
askFor: The organisation's registered status, purposes and public service information.
Latest annual-return status, accounts and trustees' report.
Whether figures include subsidiaries or cross-border reporting.
safeContactRule: The download terms prohibit direct marketing and false claims that a derivative is the Scottish Charity Register.
Do not extract trustee names or incidental people from filed PDFs.
A purpose classification belongs to the organisation and must not be used to infer a person's belief.

helpRoutes.name: Find a Northern Ireland charity through the official register

evidence.locator: Register search, CSV download, coverage and sensitive-data boundary

helpRoutes.purpose: Check a Northern Ireland registered charity's official number, status, activities, filing information and public organisation contact.
helpRoutes.firstStep: Search the official register and read CCNI's current coverage context before treating an absent result as a conclusion.
askFor: The organisation's official charity number and current own website.
Its purposes, activities, public benefit report and filing status.
The generic route for service, referral or funding questions.
safeContactRule: The register is not a complete picture of the Northern Ireland sector.
Use organisation-level public contact only after checking it is not a person's home or personal inbox.
Do not scrape trustees or filed documents to enlarge the CSV export.

helpRoutes.name: Ask a charity what help it currently offers

evidence.locator: Directory to the three territorial charity registers
Public organisation contact, website, activities and beneficiaries fields
Register fields and no-direct-marketing term
Organisation activities and public-contact search fields

helpRoutes.purpose: Turn public purpose and service information into one respectful, specific request without assuming eligibility or entitlement.
helpRoutes.firstStep: Choose the Charity Commission, OSCR or CCNI register by territory, use it to reach the charity's own current generic service, referral or contact page, then read its eligibility and safeguarding instructions.
askFor: Whether the service covers your need, location and current eligibility.
What documents, referral, appointment, costs, waiting time or accessibility arrangements apply.
Where to go next if the organisation cannot help.
safeContactRule: Send one relevant request, not bulk marketing or repeated pressure.
Share only the personal details necessary for the charity's published process and use emergency services for emergencies.
A register purpose or historic project does not guarantee current capacity, eligibility or a favourable decision.

helpRoutes.name: Use HMRC's charity tax route for recognition and relief questions

evidence.locator: Recognition conditions and application route
Reliefs, taxable income and return boundary
